topic: The August 2026 Eclipse: A Misrepresented Prediction
our_argument_summary: Section 3.2 argues that the dome's August 2026 eclipse magnetic anomaly prediction is misrepresented as a discriminating test. It claims: (1) the dome predicts −17 to −21 nT Z-component anomaly at European INTERMAGNET stations; (2) the dome falsely claims the globe predicts '0.0 nT exactly'; (3) the globe actually predicts 5–20 nT via Chapman's ionospheric mechanism (1933); (4) the dome's prediction was derived by taking GUA 2016 data and applying a 'correction factor of 1.672'; and (5) the test has three structural problems: false binary framing, Kp < 2 escape clause, and no unique signature.

holes_found.description: The '−17 to −21 nT' prediction range appears stale or oversimplified. The dome's actual predictions page (03-predictions.txt) shows a detailed dual-baseline system with 9 per-station predictions: BOU baseline gives −5.0 to −12.8 nT, W004 baseline gives −10.3 to −26.2 nT, each with station-specific ±error bars. Our text compresses this into a single '−17 to −21 nT' range that doesn't match either baseline's actual spread. A dome defender will immediately point out that we're attacking a simplified caricature of their prediction, not the actual registered prediction with per-station specificity.
holes_found.recommendation: Update the Section 3.2 prediction description to accurately reflect the dual-baseline, per-station structure. The argument that the ranges overlap with Chapman's 5–20 nT range still holds (especially for the BOU baseline, which falls entirely within Chapman's range), but the W004 baseline predictions for some stations (HAD at −26.2 nT) exceed Chapman's typical range — which should be acknowledged and addressed.

holes_found.description: Section 3.2 completely ignores the dome's four model-discriminating tests (E-PRED-A through E-PRED-D). E-PRED-B is particularly significant: it predicts that Hartland (80% coverage, FSF 1.471) will show a stronger magnetic anomaly than Ebro (95% coverage, FSF 1.029), despite lower eclipse coverage. This prediction derives from dome geometry — the FSF (Field Strength Factor) comes from H(r)/r at each station's position. The Chapman mechanism predicts signal roughly proportional to eclipse coverage (higher coverage → stronger signal → Ebro > Hartland). If Hartland exceeds Ebro, the dome has a genuine discriminating prediction that standard physics would need to explain. By not addressing E-PRED-A through D, we leave our strongest flank undefended against the dome's strongest counterargument.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a subsection addressing each E-PRED test. For E-PRED-B specifically, note that the Chapman signal depends on MORE than just coverage — local ionospheric conductivity, Sq current geometry, station latitude, and time-of-day all affect the magnitude. These standard physics factors could produce Hartland > Ebro without dome geometry. But we need to make this argument explicitly rather than ignoring the test entirely.

holes_found.description: Our text claims the dome's prediction was derived by 'applying his correction factor of 1.672' to GUA 2016 data. But 1.672 is the KAPPA magnetic-gravity coupling constant from WIN-012 (10.9 nT / 6.5 μGal), used in a completely different context. The actual eclipse formula is delta_Z = baseline × coverage × FSF, where FSF is derived from V12 H(r)/r dome geometry and varies by station (0.642 to 2.075). These are not the same number or concept. Conflating KAPPA with FSF is a factual error that a dome defender could exploit to claim we don't understand the model we're critiquing — a devastating credibility hit.
holes_found.recommendation: Correct the derivation description. The eclipse prediction uses delta_Z = baseline × coverage × FSF, where baseline is either BOU 2017 (−10.9 nT) or W004 empirical (−22.24 nT), coverage is the geometric eclipse coverage at each station, and FSF is the 'field strength factor' derived from H(r)/r. The W004 baseline (−22.24 nT) itself is the element that inflates the prediction — it's an 'empirical recalibration' from v50.2 that is roughly double the BOU measurement. Our critique should focus on the W004 baseline's provenance rather than a misidentified correction factor.

holes_found.description: Kill-Shot Test 5 cross-references Section 3.2 but discusses eclipse PATH prediction ('the prediction is derived from the same Besselian element calculations (JPL DE440/441 ephemerides) used by every eclipse predictor'), while Section 3.2 discusses eclipse MAGNETIC ANOMALY prediction. These are different arguments about different claims. A reader following the cross-reference will not find the argument Test 5 promises. The Besselian element / ephemeris argument does not appear in Section 3.2 at all.
holes_found.recommendation: Either: (a) add a paragraph to Section 3.2 addressing the eclipse path prediction and its reliance on standard ephemeris data, or (b) expand Test 5 to be self-contained with its own path-prediction analysis and remove the misleading cross-reference to Section 3.2, or (c) split Test 5 into 'path prediction' (self-contained) and 'magnetic prediction' (references 3.2).

holes_found.description: Our claim that the dome says the globe predicts '0.0 nT exactly; no physical mechanism proposed' is presented as a direct quote, but we cannot locate this exact text in the dome's predictions page (03-predictions.txt) or wins page (02-wins.txt). If this text appears on a kill-shot page we don't have in raw-text, it should be cited. If it's our paraphrase attributed as a quote, a dome defender could claim we're fabricating what they said — exactly the kind of straw-manning we accuse them of.
holes_found.recommendation: Verify the source of the '0.0 nT exactly' quote. If it's from the dome's kill-shot page, cite the specific page/section. If it's from a version no longer on the site, note that. If it's our paraphrase, reframe it as 'the site implies the globe predicts no signal' rather than quoting text that may not exist verbatim.

holes_found.description: The Kp < 2 escape clause criticism, while valid in a framing analysis, is somewhat uncharitable. Quiet-condition filtering (Kp < 2 or Kp ≤ 3) is standard practice in geomagnetic eclipse studies. Chernogor & Holub (2024) themselves applied a quiet-condition filter (Kp ≤ 3). Calling a standard methodological practice an 'escape clause' without acknowledging that mainstream science uses the same filter risks making us look like we're criticizing standard methodology when it's used by the dome but accepting it when used by mainstream researchers.
holes_found.recommendation: Acknowledge that quiet-condition filtering is standard practice, then pivot to the actual escape clause problem: the dome could retroactively declare conditions 'disturbed' after seeing unfavorable results, since Kp is a 3-hour index with significant intra-day variation. The real issue is whether the dome commits in advance to specific Kp data sources and specific time windows, or reserves the right to redefine 'quiet' after the fact.

holes_found.description: Our text says the dome 'took real INTERMAGNET data from the March 9, 2016 Pacific eclipse (station GUA: −13.16 to −15.11 nT on a quiet day).' But the dome's predictions page shows BOU 2017 (−10.9 nT) as one baseline and W004 empirical (−22.24 nT) as the other. GUA 2016 doesn't appear in the current prediction formula. Either we're describing an earlier version of the prediction, or we've confused the source station. This mismatch weakens our forensic reconstruction of how the prediction was derived.
holes_found.recommendation: Update the derivation trace to reflect the current prediction formula using BOU 2017 and W004 baselines. If GUA 2016 was the source in an earlier version, note that explicitly as version history.

stronger_arguments.description: The W004 baseline (−22.24 nT) deserves much more scrutiny. According to the evolution page (v50.2), W004 was an 'empirical recalibration' — it was originally a failed weekly test that was below the detection threshold, then repurposed as a baseline. This means the dome's stronger prediction set (W004 baseline) is built on a data point the dome itself previously classified as a noise-floor failure. The provenance chain is: failed test → relabeled as 'empirical baseline' → multiplied across 9 stations → presented as precise predictions with ±error bars. This is a much stronger attack than our current description.
sources: raw-text/02-wins.txt line 132: 'W004 — Noise Floor Limit (empirical recalibration at v50.2)'

stronger_arguments.description: The dual-baseline structure itself is a hedging strategy. The BOU baseline gives predictions in the −5 to −13 nT range (fully within Chapman's 5–20 nT range — non-discriminating). The W004 baseline gives predictions in the −10 to −26 nT range (overlapping with Chapman at the low end but exceeding it at the high end). By presenting both, the dome has a prediction that spans nearly the entire plausible range. If the observation is −10 nT, the BOU baseline 'confirms.' If it's −22 nT, the W004 baseline 'confirms.' The only failing outcome would be an observation near 0 nT (geomagnetically disturbed eclipse) — which the Kp condition already excludes. The combined prediction range (−5 to −26 nT with error bars) essentially covers all physically plausible quiet-day eclipse signals.

The FSF (Field Strength Factor) derived from H(r)/r is presented as dome-specific geometry, but H(r) is the firmament height function whose key parameter is λ_g = 8,619 km — the same fitted constant Section 3.1 already showed is one ruler used repeatedly. The FSF values across stations (0.642–2.075) should be independently derivable from published dome parameters if H(r) is published. If they are, we should verify the math. If they aren't derivable (because n(r) or other parameters are unpublished), then the FSF values are just station-specific free parameters — making the eclipse predictions an exercise in per-station curve fitting, not geometry-derived prediction.
sources: raw-text/03-predictions.txt line 22: 'FSF = field strength factor from V12 H(r)/r dome geometry'

kernel_of_truth.description: The dome model's eclipse prediction is genuinely more detailed and specific than most alternative cosmology claims. It provides 9 named station predictions with dual baselines, per-station FSF factors, specific ±error bars, and four discriminating sub-tests (E-PRED-A through D). The prediction is pre-registered with Git + OpenTimestamps anchoring. The 4 discriminating tests (especially E-PRED-B on relative station magnitudes) represent a real attempt to create falsifiable, model-specific predictions rather than generic 'the signal will exist' claims. This level of prediction specificity deserves acknowledgment even while critiquing the underlying methodology.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: Specificity is not the same as independence. The predictions are specific but not independently derived from dome geometry alone: the baselines come from globe-era INTERMAGNET data (BOU 2017, W004), the eclipse coverage comes from standard Besselian element ephemeris calculations (which assume a spherical Earth), and even the FSF factor uses H(r) whose key parameter λ_g was fitted to geomagnetic data measured on the globe model's coordinate system. The prediction is a chain of globe-sourced inputs processed through a dome-labeled formula. The pre-registration is genuine but registers a computation derived from standard data, not an independent dome-geometric derivation.

text_fingerprint.section_title: 3.2 The August 2026 Eclipse: A Misrepresented Prediction

deeper_analysis:
Section 3.2 is strategically critical because the August 2026 eclipse is the dome's headline pending test — the one most likely to generate attention when the event occurs in ~4 months. If our critique of this prediction contains factual errors about what the dome actually predicts (wrong range, wrong correction factor, wrong source station), those errors will be immediately visible when the eclipse data comes in and both communities scrutinize the prediction. We need to get this section right not just for intellectual integrity but because it will be the most-read section of our review in August 2026.

The core argument (the test is non-discriminating because Chapman predicts the same range) is sound for the BOU baseline predictions. But the W004 baseline predictions for some stations (especially HAD at −26.2 nT) exceed the typical Chapman range of 5–20 nT. If HAD actually measures −25 nT on eclipse day, the dome will claim confirmation of a result the Chapman mechanism struggles to explain at that magnitude. Our current text doesn't address this scenario.

The biggest strategic gap is ignoring E-PRED-B. The dome's argument that Hartland should beat Ebro despite lower coverage is actually the most interesting prediction in the entire eclipse suite. It's specific, it's derived from dome geometry (FSF from H(r)/r), and it makes a relative prediction that doesn't depend on the absolute baseline. Even if our absolute-range overlap argument is correct, the RELATIVE ranking of stations is a genuine test. Standard ionospheric physics predicts that eclipse magnetic effects scale with coverage fraction AND local ionospheric conductivity AND Sq current geometry AND local time — so the Hartland/Ebro ordering is not trivially predictable by either model. This is a prediction we should be analyzing rather than ignoring.

On the other hand, the dome's dual-baseline strategy IS a hedge. The BOU and W004 baselines differ by a factor of ~2, giving prediction ranges that span most of the physically plausible signal space. Combined with the Kp escape clause, the dome has constructed a prediction that is very difficult to falsify: almost any quiet-day eclipse signal from −5 to −26 nT will match one baseline. This is a strong structural critique we should be making more explicitly.
